coredata.dc:title: Length-controlled sorting and length-dependent properties of short semiconducting single-walled carbon nanotubes
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yutao
Li, Yahui
Wen, Haijian
Cao, Leitao
Xiao, Wenxin
Zhao, Jintao
Nan, Zeyuan
Zhang, Suxia
Qiu, Song
Li, Qingwen
coredata.dc:description: Single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) are characteristic one-dimensional nanomaterials, with length as their defining feature. However, the exploration of length-dependent properties in semiconducting-SWCNTs (s-SWCNTs) has hitherto been underexplored, primarily due to the absence of precise preparation technologies to obtain s-SWCNTs with both controlled length and narrow length distribution. Here, we introduced a novel approach based on electrochemical shortening and selective adsorption, offering control over the SWCNTs' length and their distributions. This methodology is compatible with high-purity SWCNTs sorting techniques. We have conducted detailed length statistics and comparisons of the truncation results of SWCNTs with different diameters. Experimental results demonstrate that smaller-diameter SWCNTs s are more prone to truncation, resulting in shorter lengths. Remarkably, we observed a length-dependent fluorescence enhancement when the length of s-SWCNTs was reduced to less than 120 nm, akin to the behavior of carbon dots or conjugated polymers. This research paves the way for exploring more length-dependent properties and length-related applications of s-SWCNTs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Semiconducting single-walled carbon nanotubes
Length-dependent
Truncation
Fluorescence enhancement
Length sorting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dispersion process of SWCNTs in water
Electrolytic truncation of the SWCNTs aqueous dispersion
Controllable adsorption of SWCNTs by silica microspheres
Thin film preparation procedure for characterization
Length-controlled truncation and sorting of s-SWCNTs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Classification of SWCNTs dispersions at different stages. (a) Experimental flow from raw SWCNTs to short s-SWCNTs. The experimental procedure consists of three steps. The dispersion obtained by performing only step A and step C is named Control (b) SEM image of the thin film deposited from a SWCNTs aqueous dispersion (Aq). (c) SEM image of the thin film deposited from organic s-SWCNTs dispersion. (d) (e) Absorption spectra of SWCNTs in aqueous and organic dispersions before and after semiconducting sorting. The M11 region represents the characteristic peak of m-SWCNTs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Length statistics of SWCNTs with different diameters. (a) Schematic diagram of the truncation and sorting process. (b) (c) (d) AFM images of HiPCO, Arc, and Tuball SWCNTs with a scale bar of 300 nm. (e) Box plots of the length distributions of the three kinds of SWCNTs after truncation. (f) The Bar chart shows the median length change of the three types of s-SWCNTs before and after truncation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Length statistics of s-SWCNTs under different Electrolysis Duration, (a) Schematic diagram of the treatment process with regulated electrolysis times of 2/4/8 hours. (b) AFM image of the Control group, scale bar is 1 μm. (c) Length distribution of the Control group, with the solid line representing the Gaussian fitting curve. The FWHM represents the full width at half maximum, and the LM is 658 nm. (d) (f) (h) AFM images of s-SWCNTs after 2/4/8 h of electrolysis, scale bar is 200 nm. (e) (g) (i) Length distribution of s-SWCNTs after electrolysis for 2/4/8 hours, with the LM being 267/120/86 nm, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Process of adsorption of long s-SWCNTs on silica microspheres to obtain ultrashort s-SWCNTs dispersion. (a) Schematic diagram of s-SWCNTs adsorption on silica microspheres. (b) SEM image of the silica microsphere surface with many adsorbed SWCNTs. (c) The AFM phase diagram and height diagram of ultra-short s-SWCNTs (SA-8h). (d) The height profile of SA-8h s-SWCNTs. (e) Length distribution of the EC-8h and SA-8h, with solid lines indicating the fitted Gaussian curves. (f) Box plots and FWHM variation curves of s-SWCNTs of different lengths, with horizontal lines and stars in the boxes representing median and LM lengths, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Length-dependent fluorescence properties of s-SWCNTs. (a) PLE images of s-SWCNTs of different lengths and the white dashed line is the emission region passing through the excitation wavelength of 730 nm. The excitation spectra of the PLE are in the range of 1000–1300 nm, and the emission spectra are in the range of 600–800 nm. (b) PL spectra of s-SWCNTs with different lengths at an excitation wavelength of 730 nm. (c) The plot of s-SWCNTs length versus fluorescence intensity, with different chiralities represented by lines of different colors, and the individual points corresponding to the maximum fluorescence intensity of each chirality species. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

simple-head.ce:title: Length-controlled sorting and length-dependent properties of short semiconducting single-walled carbon nanotubes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) are characteristic one-dimensional nanomaterials, with length as their defining feature. However, the exploration of length-dependent properties in semiconducting-SWCNTs (s-SWCNTs) has hitherto been underexplored, primarily due to the absence of precise preparation technologies to obtain s-SWCNTs with both controlled length and narrow length distribution. Here, we introduced a novel approach based on electrochemical shortening and selective adsorption, offering control over the SWCNTs' length and their distributions. This methodology is compatible with high-purity SWCNTs sorting techniques. We have conducted detailed length statistics and comparisons of the truncation results of SWCNTs with different diameters. Experimental results demonstrate that smaller-diameter SWCNTs s are more prone to truncation, resulting in shorter lengths. Remarkably, we observed a length-dependent fluorescence enhancement when the length of s-SWCNTs was reduced to less than 120 nm, akin to the behavior of carbon dots or conjugated polymers. This research paves the way for exploring more length-dependent properties and length-related applications of s-SWCNTs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) possess superior mechanical, thermal, optical, and electrical attributes, finding extensive utility in electronics, composite materials, and biomedicine [1–7]. The precise preparation of SWCNTs has been the epicenter of research, with diameter, and chirality being the primary focus [8]. Length, a cardinal structural property of SWCNTs, when amplified, enables SWCNTs to extrapolate their remarkable mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties to a micrometer or even centimeter scales, with substantial advancements made in this research domain [9–11]. Contrastingly, investigations related to ultra-short SWCNTs are relatively sparse.

Presently, most commercial raw SWCNTs exhibit lengths concentrated below a few micrometers with a broad length distribution. Shortening the length of SWCNTs can unlock some unique applications. Firstly, isolated single-chiral SWCNTs can serve as seeds for continued clonal growth, facilitating the high-purity batch preparation of single-chiral SWCNTs [12,13]. Secondly, as the length of SWCNTs decreases, their cytotoxicity diminishes, enabling easier metabolism within the body. Hartman et al. employed fluorination and thermolytic chemical cutting techniques to reduce the length of SWCNTs to 20–80 nm. They subsequently introduced paramagnetic Ga3+ ions into the inner cavity, resulting in the formation of ion clusters. These Ga-SWCNTs demonstrate exceptional biocompatibility and signal transmission efficiency, positioning them as novel contrast agents for biological imaging [14]. Furthermore, shorter SWCNTs, functioning as nanoscale containers, are better suited for incorporating other functional materials, thereby imparting novel properties such as catalytic, luminescent, and magnetic characteristics. Finally, when truncated to below 50 nm, SWCNTs exhibit properties akin to carbon dots, representing a transition from 1-dimensional to 0-dimensional materials. This transition gives rise to “quantum size effects” and exceptional optical properties, which find applications in fluorescent labeling, sensor arrays, and other related fields [7,15]. However, the fluorescence of short SWCNTs is significantly affected by the quenching effect of metallic SWCNTs and impurities [16,17]. Moreover, the wide length distribution of semiconducting SWCNTs hampers the comprehensive exploration of specific length-dependent properties. Within a certain length range distribution, the fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quantum yield decrease with length [18–20]. Consequently, to investigate the length-dependent characteristics of short SWCNTs, it becomes imperative to obtain a narrow distribution of ultrashort s-SWCNTs.

There have been many attempts to truncate CNTs. According to whether new chemical bonds are formed, these methods can be divided into physical cutting methods and chemical methods [21–24]. Physical methods such as ball milling and ultrasonic methods can usually obtain large quantities of short tubes but may damage the CNTs’ structure. Chemical methods such as strong acid oxidation and fluorination can achieve even shorter lengths, but they introduce new groups to the tube walls and ends, thereby affecting the performance of CNTs. Most methods for truncating CNTs (whether physical or chemical) have a certain limit on the length of the obtained short CNTs. For example, it is often possible to obtain short CNTs (200–400 nm) but difficult to obtain large quantities of ultra-short CNTs (<100 nm), and the short CNTs obtained still have a wide length distribution. Electrochemical methods use current to generate hydroxyl radicals, convert sp2 bonds on the CNTs surface to sp3 bonds, create defects, and destroy them to achieve the purpose of truncation. Zhang et al. used a secondary electrolysis method to electrolyze multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and obtained short MWCNTs [25]. However, SWCNTs are often more prone to bundling than MWCNTs, making the efficiency and final length of electrolytic truncation not comparable to MWCNTs. The above methods generally result in a wide distribution of truncated SWCNTs. Some ultra-short SWCNTs are often separated by chromatographic column separation or density gradient centrifugation, which is complex and can lead to a large loss of product. At the same time, these truncation techniques are generally incompatible with semiconducting SWCNTs sorting techniques, making it difficult to characterize the properties of s-SWCNTs of different lengths.

This paper presents a novel approach utilizing electrochemical shortening and selective adsorption to achieve precise control over the length and distribution of s-SWCNTs. Our study focuses on investigating the variations in the truncation process for SWCNTs with different diameters and species. To comprehensively understand the optical performance differences of SWCNTs based on their lengths, we conducted in-depth spectral testing and characterization on a series of short s-SWCNTs with varying lengths and length distributions. This work not only fills a critical gap in the fabrication of narrow-distribution ultra-short s-SWCNTs but also provides a good foundation for exploring the length-dependent properties of SWCNTs and expands the possibilities for their length-related applications.
The experimental materials and equipment are comprehensively elucidated in the Supporting Information with meticulous detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion process of SWCNTs in water
ce:section.ce:para: 10 mg of raw SWCNTs powder was mixed with 10 mg of sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) in water and the mixture was sonicated for 2 hours using an apical sonicator (Sonics VC500) with an amplitude of 20%. The entire sonication process was carried out in a thermostatic water bath at a temperature of about 6 °C to obtain an aqueous dispersion of SWCNTs, Aq.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrolytic truncation of the SWCNTs aqueous dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental setup is shown in Fig. S1. 80 ml of deionized water was mixed with 0.2 ml of concentrated sulfuric acid to prepare a dilute sulfuric acid electrolyte. The dispersed water dispersion was added to the electrolyte. A constant current power supply was used with a current of 0.5 A. Mechanical stirring was continuously performed during the electrolysis at a rate of 1000 r/min. Hydrogen gas was generated near the electrodes during the electrolysis, and with the stirring and electrolysis process, a large amount of foam was produced from the bottom, taking away some of the bottom-settled tube bundles. After a certain period of electrolysis, the remaining dispersion in the electrolytic cell was collected to obtain a water dispersion of SWCNTs with truncated lengths.

The obtained aqueous dispersion of SWCNTs was vacuum filtered through 0.1 μm cellulose acetate membrane. The membrane was washed with a large amount of deionized water to pH neutrality and then continued with a large amount of toluene solvent. After washing, the membrane was dried to obtain truncated SWCNTs film [26]. The carbon film and 10 mg of the polymer [9-(1-octylononyl)-9H-carbazole (PCz)] were dispersed in a 20 ml volume of toluene solvent using a probe sonicator to sonicate for 15 minutes at 30% amplitude. The centrifugation process was performed at 20,000 g for 1 h. After centrifugation, 90% of the supernatant was collected to obtain a high-purity s-SWCNTs organic dispersion EC (Electrolytic Cutting) [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Controllable adsorption of SWCNTs by silica microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: The s-SWCNTs obtained were added to 10 mg of non-porous silica microspheres with a particle size of 10 μm and stirred at 1000 r/min for 45 min [28]. After centrifugation at 5000g for 30 minutes, the remaining short s-SWCNTs (post-Silica microsphere adsorption, SA) was collected from the supernatant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thin film preparation procedure for characterization
ce:section.ce:para: 4-inch Si/SiO2 wafers were placed in flat-bottomed glassware, 2 ml of the organic dispersion to be deposited was added, and then 18 ml of toluene was added to dilute the dispersion. After one hour of deposition on a hotplate at a constant temperature of 80 °C, the completed wafers were taken out, washed with toluene and acetone, and dried by a nitrogen gun, resulting in the sparse SWCNTs thin films. These films are then subjected to AFM and SEM measurements to assess length and topographic characteristics. The scan rate of AFM is 1.51 Hz, the amplitude set point is maintained between 250 and 300 mv. The drive amplitude is set around 1000 mv. The emission wavelength of PL spectrum is 900 nm–1600 nm. The emission range used for PL spectra was set between 900 nm and 1600 nm, while PLE spectra were recorded within the range of 900 nm–1300 nm. Excitation wavelengths for PLE measurements were selected between 600 nm and 800 nm. The slit width and Increment are 20 and 15 nm, respectively, and the integration time is 0.5 s. The slit width and Inclement are 20 and 15 nm, respectively, and the integration time is 0.5 s. In each sample, the length of 150 SWCNTs is measured and counted at a minimum of five different locations on the thin film.

The density SWCNTs thin-film samples for Raman spectroscopy measurement were prepared by drop-casting the organic dispersions onto Si/SiO2 substrates. Raman spectroscopy was performed at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm, with a magnification of × 20 and a spot size of 5 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Length-controlled truncation and sorting of s-SWCNTs
ce:section.ce:para: We initiated our study by dispersing raw SWCNTs in solvent water with the surfactant SDS. This process yielded a monodisperse aqueous dispersion (Aq) exhibiting excellent uniformity (Fig. 1 a). Ultrasonic dispersion was instrumental in decreasing the length of SWCNTs by causing partial disruption of their inherent defects [29]. The following process of electrolysis in acidic solutions (Fig. S1) produced hydroxyl radicals and created active reaction sites on the wall of SWCNTs, which method is renowned for its efficacy in severing CNTs [30]. Under the applied electric current, CNTs fractured at defects and active sites, leading to truncation. Hydroxyl radicals selectively oxidized carbon atoms on the CNT walls, creating new defects. The magnetic stirring combined with the much-rising foam generated during the electrolysis process effectively enhanced the exchange of SWCNTs on the electrode surface and improved the entire truncation efficiency.

After the electrolysis process, all semiconducting SWCNTs (s-SWCNTs), metallic SWCNTs (m-SWCNTs) and amorphous carbon can be stably dispersed in the SDS aqueous dispersion. As shown in Fig. 1b, the thin film deposited from aqueous dispersions usually contain a large number of amorphous carbon particles and surfactant aggregates. The process of water de-wetting tends to trigger the ‘coffee ring effect’ during the film formation, causing most SWCNTs to exist in clusters or bundles within the film. These factors can greatly interfere with the characterization of the SWCNTs, especially for ultra-short SWCNTs. Therefore, we removed most of the surfactants on the surface of the SWCNTs through a gentle filtration and washing process. Then, we used a very mature and reliable organic separation technology with the conjugated polymer PCz to sort out the s-SWCNTs from the mixed SWCNTs in toluene. This organic dispersion that was electrolytically truncated was named EC. As shown in Fig. 1c, the s-SWCNTs in the SEM image formed by the deposition of EC dispersion are evenly distributed, with clean surfaces, no obvious impurities, and no agglomeration, which is very beneficial for length measurement and statistics. Correspondingly, we also subjected the Aq dispersion that had not undergone the electrolytic shortening process to the same filtration, cleaning, and organic sorting process, and the resulting organic dispersion was used as the Control group for subsequent comparisons.

According to our past work, PCz can achieve semiconducting sorting with a purity of over 99.99% [27,31]. As can be seen by comparing Fig. 1d and e, the M11 metal peak in the solution has almost completely disappeared. The comparison of the Raman spectra in Fig. S2 [32] and the absorption spectra of the Arc s-SWCNTs can further prove the effective removal of m-SWCNTs. Since m-SWCNTs can quench the fluorescence excitons, the presence of even a small amount of m-SWCNTs can have a huge impact on the fluorescence property testing of SWCNTs dispersions. The high-purity s-SWCNTs of different lengths are very beneficial for the subsequent characterization of fluorescence properties.

Fig. 2 illustrates how the variations in the diameter of raw SWCNTs affect the length of the resulting SWCNTs after the process of truncation and sorting. In order to maximize the deposition of SWCNTs in solution, it is imperative to use proper temperature and long deposition duration. This strategy ensures an efficient and thorough deposition of SWCNTs. The experiment involved three kinds of commercial SWCNTs with different diameter ranges: HiPCO (0.8–1.2 nm), arc-discharged (1.3–1.7 nm), and Tuball (1.0–2.0 nm). The dispersion of these SWCNTs was subjected to identical truncation and sorting processes. AFM images (Fig. 2b, c, and d) revealed that HiPCO s-SWCNTs with the smallest diameters contained more short tubes, while the Arc and Tuball s-SWCNTs with larger diameters showed noticeably longer lengths (Fig. S3). Statistical analysis of tube lengths for the three different diameters is presented in Fig. 2e, revealing a positive correlation between the median length and the distribution interval of SWCNTs and the diameter of the tubes. Fig. 2f shows the length change trend before and after the truncation of different raw material tubes, reflecting the impact of diameter variation on length. These findings suggested that, under identical truncation conditions, SWCNTs with larger diameters exhibited lower truncation efficiency and wider length distribution intervals.

In response to this phenomenon, we have formulated the following hypotheses and conclusions: Firstly, it is hypothesized that SWCNTs exhibit variations in the density of surface defects. The research conducted by Ding et al. effectively illustrates the impact of surface defects on the mechanical properties of CNTs [33]. During the growth process, SWCNTs with larger diameters possess a greater surface area, leading to a higher number of defects on their tube walls compared to tubes with smaller diameters. Consequently, large-diameter tubes are more prone to truncation, resulting in a greater quantity of short tubes. However, empirical observations demonstrate that small-diameter tubes have lower lengths and length distributions than their large-diameter counterparts, indicating that intrinsic defect density is not the primary factor influencing the length dependence on diameter. Additionally, differences in charge transfer during the electrochemical process are attributed to the total charge. Kuwahara et al. have discovered that smaller-diameter tubes exhibit enhanced electrical conductivity [34]. Within the same electrolysis period, small-diameter SWCNTs accumulate a greater charge compared to larger-diameter tubes. This can be attributed to the higher specific surface area of small-diameter tubes, which facilitates accelerated chemical reactions on their surfaces. Consequently, a larger quantity of ions can be adsorbed, resulting in increased charge accumulation. Moreover, the impact of oxidative substances on SWCNTs is contingent on their diameters. Specifically, small-diameter tubes are more susceptible to damage and defect formation. The presence of a strongly acidic electrolyte facilitates oxidation of the tube walls, leading to defect formation. The work conducted by Kim et al. further confirms this phenomenon, as acid treatment preferentially shortens smaller-diameter SWCNTs during gel electrophoresis [35]. Small-diameter SWCNTs have relatively higher reactivity, making them more susceptible to defects and truncation compared to their larger-diameter counterparts.

We employ HiPCO s-SWCNTs to investigate the correlation between the truncated length of SWCNTs and the electrolysis time. AFM images in Fig. 3 revealed the different stages of the tubes, from the single long tube distribution in the Control group to mostly short tubes in the EC-8h group. The length distribution of SWCNTs in the dispersions with varying electrolysis times was conducted with statistics, and 150 SWCNT samples were counted for each group (Fig. 3e, g, 3i). As shown in Fig. S4, at the initial stage of truncation, the statistical curve of s-SWCNTs length presented a symmetric normal distribution, and the mode length (LM, the value that appears most frequently in statistics), median length, and mean length approximations, can be used to assess the length of s-SWCNTs. However, as the length gradually decreases, the length distribution is no longer symmetrical, and the long-tail phenomena become more and more obvious. At this time, the median and mean length cannot accurately evaluate the length of the s-SWCNTs, while the LM has better accuracy and reproducibility in evaluating and comparing the experimental process.

As revealed in Fig. 3c, the length statistics of s-SWCNTs in the control group displayed a fairly broad Gaussian distribution. After two hours of electrolysis, the LM witnessed a significant decrease from 658 nm to 267 nm, by a reduction in the full width at half maximum (FWHM) from 605 nm to 277 nm (Fig. 3e). As the electrolysis time increased, the decrease of LM becomes more and more obvious. The LM reaches 86 nm after 8 hours, and the length distribution is further narrowed to 151 nm, with an obvious long-tailed distribution. At this point, the Gaussian method is no longer able to provide an accurately assessable distribution. Instead, the lognormal distribution curves were utilized to fit the long tail part in EC-4h & EC-8h [36].

The above results demonstrated that electrolysis could effectively control both the length and length distribution simultaneously. During the electrolysis process, the energy applied to the SWCNTs defects resulted in the generation of hydroxyl radicals, which induced truncation of the tube [37]. As the SWCNTs' length shortened within the electrolysis cycle, the probability of free radicals generating defects on the tube walls declined, possibly due to reduced contact between the tube walls and free radicals.

To further narrow the length distribution of ultra-short s-SWCNTs, we adopted the methodology using silica microsphere adsorption to remove medium-length tubes [28]. Fig. 4 a illustrates the process of silica microsphere with specific diameter adsorption of s-SWCNTs. When the length distribution of s-SWCNTs falls within a certain range, these s-SWCNTs with corresponding lengths can be adsorbed on a certain curved surface of silica with strong enough interaction forces to avoid detachment. As shown in Fig. 4b, the SEM image of the silica microsphere surface shows a lot of deposited s-SWCNTs, indicating the effective adsorption of s-SWCNTs using a silica microsphere.

Further characterization of ultra-short s-SWCNTs was carried out through AFM, as shown in Fig. 4c s-SWCNTs smaller than 60 nm in the field of view already occupy a large proportion, and the images of these ultra-short s-SWCNTs generally blurry. It is necessary to use both phase and height phase comparisons to effectively search. The shortest s-SWCNTs visible is about 30 nm, and shorter s-SWCNTs can only be faintly seen, but cannot be seen simultaneously in the Phase and Height phases. To confirm whether the tubes we counted were s-SWCNTs, we conducted transverse sections perpendicular to the major axis of all tubes included in the statistics. Taking Fig. 4d as an example, we only included tubes with a height between 1 and 2 nm in the sample. Combined with the Raman spectrum image in Fig. S5, the gradual increase in the D band also indirectly proves the decrease in s-SWCNTs length.

The length statistics of EC-8h and SA-8h indicate that after the adsorption process, a significant decline in SWCNTs longer than 100 nm was observed, and nearly all SWCNTs longer than 250 nm vanished. Finally, the LM of SA-8h decreased to 61 nm with an FWHM of 85 nm., summarizing in Fig. 4f, we obtained five batches of high-purity short s-SWCNTs dispersions with different lengths and length distributions.

HiPCO s-SWCNTs with different lengths were also investigated for their optical absorption and fluorescence properties. As shown in Fig. S6, within a wide range, the concentration and absorption intensity of all the chiral species in the Control group show a standard linear relationship. According to the Lambert-Beer law, under specific concentration ranges with other conditions unchanged, an increase in absorption intensity is always accompanied by an increase in molar concentration. At the same time, the intensity of the absorption also corresponds to the number of carbon atoms in dispersion [38].

As shown in Fig. S7, the absorption spectra of four kinds of s-SWCNTs with different lengths remain consistent in most regions, with significant differences only at the combined peaks of (8,7) and (11,1) species at wavelengths around 1280 nm. We adjusted the concentration of the four kinds of s-SWCNTs dispersions to ensure that all the absorption spectra are basically the same at 1140 nm where the (7,6) SWCNTs are located, which will facilitate the subsequent comparison of fluorescence spectra of these dispersions.

The fluorescence spectra of these s-SWCNTs dispersions were measured as shown in Fig. 5 . A constant dark count value was subtracted from all fluorescence tests to ensure accurate and reliable spectral analysis. To obtain the dark count value, we analyzed the spectrum of empty toluene solvent prior to testing. Fitting this spectrum plot gives the dark count value. From the scale bar of the Photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra, which can be seen that with the increase of electrolysis time and the reduction of s-SWCNTs length, the fluorescence intensity (FI) of the main chiral spices in the dispersions becomes stronger and stronger (Fig. 5a). The fluorescence spectra at the excitation wavelengths of 660 nm (Fig. S8) and 730 nm (Fig. 5b) were selected for the subsequent analysis and comparison, which cover the main chiral spices ((7,5), (7,6), (8,6) and (8,7)) in the s-SWCNTs dispersions. In these fluorescence spectra, two trends can be found from bottom to top (EC-2h, EC-4h, EC-8h, and SA-8h): first, as the length of s-SWCNTs decreased, the fluorescence intensities of the different chiral species enhanced significantly. Second, in the fluorescence spectra of the EC-2h and EC-4h, the baseline noise is very obvious. However, as the length further decreased, the curves of EC-8h and SA-8h became smooth. Furthermore, we compared the fluorescence spectra of these four kinds of s-SWCNTs together with the control group. The plot is according to the FI of different chiral species and the length of corresponding groups (LM). As shown in Fig. 5c and Fig. S8c, the control group has the longest length and the strongest FI (Fig. S10). As the length gradually decreases, the EC-2h and EC-4h groups have the lowest FI. Interestingly, when the length is shortened to below 120 nm, the FI begins to increase. There was a regular linear relationship between length and FI in EC-8h and SA-8h.

Our comprehensive review of the literature reveals a consensus that the fluorescence intensity of SWCNTs decreases proportionally with their decreasing length. No previous studies ever reported our observations of length-dependent fluorescence enhancement. In addition, our analysis of the spectrum reveals a slight blue shift for a few nanometers, which is in line with the observations reported by Dai's research team [39]. This discrepancy led us to identify two significant limitations in previous research: first, the lack of thorough semiconducting sorting for SWCNTs [21], given that the strong fluorescence-quenching properties of m-SWCNTs, even a minute remnant of m-SWCNTs can significantly disrupt fluorescence measurement. Second, the inherent difficulty in accurately measuring and analyzing the lengths of ultra-short SWCNTs [19,20]. Past studies have generally concentrated on tube lengths in excess of 100 nm, while limited observations below this threshold exhibited wide length distributions. These factors impede the independent characterizing the properties of ultra-short SWCNTs. The mechanisms underlying the anomalous fluorescence enhancement observed in s-SWCNTs are not yet fully understood. We propose that a crucial prerequisite for unraveling this enigma is the attainment of high-purity semiconducting sorting and ultra-narrow diameter distribution sorting. Additionally, the fluorescence of s-SWCNTs can be effectively protected through the encapsulation of conjugated polymers or conjugated molecules.

In summary, the present research has devised a synergistic methodology based on electrochemical truncation and selective adsorption with size-specific silica microspheres. This methodology is compatible with the high-purity sorting process of s-SWCNTs, enabling controlled preparation and efficient modulation of their length and length distribution. We have conducted detailed length statistics and comparisons of the truncated s-SWCNTs. Experimental results demonstrate that smaller-diameter SWCNTs s are more prone to truncation, resulting in shorter mean lengths. Remarkably, the LM of HiPCO s-SWCNTs can be reduced to a minimal 61nm, with the lowest FWHM being tapered to 85 nm. Furthermore, we have conducted in-depth research on the length-dependent optical properties of these short s-SWCNTs. When the lengths of s-SWCNTs are reduced to below 120 nm, their photoluminescence intensity no longer decreases but instead exhibits a length-dependent fluorescence enhancement. This investigation lays the groundwork for the study of the diverse length-dependent characteristics of SWCNTs and paves the way for the advancement of applications of ultra-short SWCNTs in domains such as fluorescent imaging, and nano-embedded structure.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFA0714700), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22075312).
